Equal to the actual frequency emitted by the siren
Higher than the actual frequency emitted by the siren
Alternating between higher and lower than the actual frequency
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Higher than the actual frequency emitted by the siren

The Doppler effect states that when a sound source moves toward an observer, the observed frequency is higher than the emitted frequency. This occurs because each successive wavefront is emitted from a position closer to the observer, effectively compressing the wavelength and increasing the frequency perceived.

fobserved = fsource x (vsound)/(vsound - vsource)

Since vsource is subtracted in the denominator (source approaching), the denominator is smaller than the numerator, making fobserved > fsource.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Lower than the actual frequency: A lower perceived frequency (redshift in sound) occurs when the source is moving away from the observer. As the ambulance passes and recedes, the observer would then perceive a drop in frequency. This explains the familiar pitch change as an ambulance drives by.

B. Equal to the actual frequency: The perceived frequency equals the emitted frequency only when there is no relative motion between source and observer along the line connecting them (or when the source moves perpendicular to that line at the closest approach).

D. Alternating between higher and lower: The frequency does not alternate. It is consistently higher while approaching and consistently lower while receding. The abrupt apparent change in pitch occurs at the moment the ambulance passes the observer, but at any given instant, the frequency is either higher or lower than the true value -- never oscillating.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Doppler effect applies to all waves, not just sound. In light, approaching sources appear blueshifted (higher frequency) and receding sources appear redshifted (lower frequency).
  • In medicine, Doppler ultrasound uses this principle to measure blood flow velocity: blood flowing toward the transducer reflects higher-frequency echoes.
  • The Doppler shift does not change the speed of the wave in the medium, only the perceived frequency and wavelength.

Bottom Line: Approaching source = higher perceived frequency (wavefronts compressed). Receding source = lower perceived frequency (wavefronts stretched). This is the Doppler effect, used in medical ultrasound, radar, and astronomy.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: If the frequency of a sound wave is doubled, what happens to its period?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: The period is halved

Frequency (f) and period (T) are inversely related:

T = 1/f   or equivalently   f = 1/T

If frequency doubles (f → 2f), the period becomes: T' = 1/(2f) = T/2. The period is halved.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. The period doubles: This would require a direct proportionality between frequency and period, which contradicts their inverse relationship. If frequency increases, period must decrease. Doubling the period would actually require halving the frequency.

C. The period remains unchanged: Period and frequency are fundamentally linked; changing one necessarily changes the other. They can only remain unchanged simultaneously. This answer might be confused with the behavior of wavelength in a different context (e.g., standing waves in a fixed medium).

D. The period is quadrupled: This would imply T is proportional to f2, which is not the case. The inverse relationship means if f is multiplied by any factor n, T is divided by the same factor n.

Key Educational Points:

  • Period is the time for one complete oscillation (measured in seconds). Frequency is the number of complete oscillations per second (measured in hertz, Hz).
  • The wave equation connects frequency, wavelength, and speed: v = f x λ. If frequency doubles in the same medium (constant v), the wavelength is halved.
  • This inverse relationship applies to all periodic phenomena: sound waves, electromagnetic waves, pendulums, and any oscillatory motion.

Bottom Line: T = 1/f. Doubling frequency halves the period. Tripling frequency gives one-third the period. Frequency and period are always inversely proportional -- they are reciprocals of each other.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: An N-type semiconductor is created by doping a silicon crystal with atoms from which group of the periodic table?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Group VA (e.g., phosphorus, arsenic)

N-type semiconductors are produced by doping silicon (Group IVA, 4 valence electrons) with elements from Group VA (5 valence electrons), such as phosphorus, arsenic, or antimony. These donor atoms contribute four electrons to covalent bonds with silicon and have one extra electron that is loosely bound and easily freed to become a conduction electron. The "N" stands for negative, referring to the excess negative charge carriers (electrons).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Group IIIA (e.g., boron, gallium): Group IIIA elements have only 3 valence electrons. When they substitute for silicon, they create an electron deficiency (a "hole") in the crystal lattice. This produces a P-type semiconductor, where the majority charge carriers are positive holes. These dopants are called acceptors because they accept electrons from neighboring bonds.

C. Group IA (e.g., sodium, potassium): Alkali metals with 1 valence electron are not used as semiconductor dopants. They do not substitute properly into the silicon crystal lattice (wrong size and bonding characteristics) and would disrupt the tetrahedral crystal structure rather than creating controlled n-type or p-type regions.

D. Group VIIA (e.g., chlorine, bromine): Halogens with 7 valence electrons are not standard semiconductor dopants. They are highly electronegative and reactive, and they do not integrate into the silicon lattice in the controlled manner needed for semiconductor doping.

Key Educational Points:

  • N-type: Group VA dopant (donor) → extra electron → majority carriers are electrons.
  • P-type: Group IIIA dopant (acceptor) → electron hole → majority carriers are holes.
  • The dopant concentrations are extremely small (about 1 dopant per 106 to 108 silicon atoms), yet they dramatically change the conductivity.
  • Combining N-type and P-type regions creates a PN junction, the basis for diodes, transistors, and all modern electronics.

Bottom Line: N-type = Group VA dopant (5 valence electrons, 1 extra → donor → excess electrons). P-type = Group IIIA dopant (3 valence electrons, 1 missing → acceptor → excess holes). Silicon is Group IVA with 4 valence electrons.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: When a PN junction diode is connected in forward bias, which of the following occurs?
options: The depletion region widens and current flow is negligible
The diode has high resistance and blocks current flow
The depletion region narrows and a relatively large current flows
Only holes can cross the junction while electrons are blocked
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: The depletion region narrows and a relatively large current flows

In forward bias, the positive terminal of the battery is connected to the P-side and the negative terminal to the N-side. This arrangement:

  • Pushes holes from the P-side toward the junction
  • Pushes electrons from the N-side toward the junction
  • Narrows the depletion region by reducing the potential barrier
  • Allows large current to flow once the threshold voltage is exceeded (~0.7 V for silicon, ~0.3 V for germanium)

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. The depletion region widens and current flow is negligible: This describes reverse bias, not forward bias. In reverse bias, the external voltage reinforces the built-in potential, widening the depletion region and preventing significant current flow (only a tiny leakage current passes).

B. The diode has high resistance and blocks current flow: Again, this is the behavior under reverse bias. In forward bias, the diode's effective resistance drops dramatically once the threshold voltage is reached, allowing substantial current flow.

D. Only holes can cross the junction while electrons are blocked: In forward bias, both charge carriers contribute to current flow. Holes move from P to N across the junction, while electrons simultaneously move from N to P. The conventional current direction is from P to N (anode to cathode).

Key Educational Points:

  • Forward bias: external voltage opposes the built-in potential → depletion region shrinks → low resistance → large current.
  • Reverse bias: external voltage reinforces the built-in potential → depletion region expands → high resistance → negligible current.
  • The diode acts as a one-way valve for current: it conducts easily in forward bias and blocks in reverse bias. This property is used for rectification (converting AC to DC).

Bottom Line: Forward bias (P to +, N to -) narrows the depletion region, reduces resistance, and allows large current flow. Reverse bias does the opposite. The threshold voltage for silicon diodes is approximately 0.7 V.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A PN junction diode under reverse bias exhibits:
options: Low resistance and large forward current
A narrowed depletion region and increased conductivity
High resistance and only a very small leakage current
Zero resistance due to quantum tunneling effects
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: High resistance and only a very small leakage current

In reverse bias, the positive terminal is connected to the N-side and the negative terminal to the P-side. This pulls majority carriers away from the junction: electrons in the N-region are pulled toward the positive terminal, and holes in the P-region are pulled toward the negative terminal. The result:

  • The depletion region widens substantially
  • The potential barrier increases
  • The diode presents very high resistance
  • Only a tiny reverse saturation current (leakage current) flows, due to thermally generated minority carriers

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Low resistance and large forward current: This describes forward bias behavior. Under reverse bias, the resistance is very high (megaohms or higher) and the current is negligible (typically nanoamperes to microamperes for silicon diodes).

B. A narrowed depletion region and increased conductivity: The depletion region widens under reverse bias, not narrows. The widened depletion region acts as an insulating layer, decreasing conductivity rather than increasing it.

D. Zero resistance due to quantum tunneling effects: Quantum tunneling does occur in heavily doped PN junctions (Zener diodes) at specific reverse voltages (Zener breakdown), but this is a specialized case, not the general behavior. Normal reverse-biased diodes have very high resistance, not zero resistance.

Key Educational Points:

  • If the reverse voltage exceeds the breakdown voltage, the diode can conduct heavily in reverse (avalanche or Zener breakdown). This is destructive for normal diodes but is the operating principle of Zener diodes (used for voltage regulation).
  • The reverse leakage current approximately doubles for every 10 degrees C increase in temperature, since it depends on thermally generated minority carriers.
  • The widened depletion region under reverse bias increases the diode's capacitance area, which is exploited in varactor (varicap) diodes for tuning circuits.

Bottom Line: Reverse bias widens the depletion region, creates high resistance, and allows only a tiny leakage current. The diode effectively blocks current in this configuration, which is the basis for its rectifying behavior.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A half-wave rectifier converts an AC input into a DC output by:
options: Allowing both positive and negative half-cycles to pass through
Allowing only one half-cycle (positive or negative) to pass while blocking the other
Doubling the frequency of the input signal
Inverting the negative half-cycle to produce a smooth constant voltage
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Allowing only one half-cycle (positive or negative) to pass while blocking the other

A half-wave rectifier uses a single diode to convert AC to pulsating DC. During the forward-biased half-cycle, the diode conducts and current flows through the load. During the reverse-biased half-cycle, the diode blocks current and the output is zero. The result is a pulsating waveform that contains only positive (or only negative) half-cycles.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Allowing both positive and negative half-cycles to pass through: If both half-cycles passed, the output would still be AC, not DC. Allowing both half-cycles (by inverting one) describes a full-wave rectifier, which uses either a center-tapped transformer with two diodes or a bridge of four diodes to utilize both halves of the AC cycle.

C. Doubling the frequency of the input signal: A half-wave rectifier does not change the frequency. The output pulses have the same frequency as the input AC. It is the full-wave rectifier whose output pulses at twice the input frequency (because it converts both half-cycles into positive pulses).

D. Inverting the negative half-cycle to produce a smooth constant voltage: A half-wave rectifier does not invert the blocked half-cycle; it simply eliminates it (output = 0 during that time). Producing a smooth constant voltage requires additional filtering (typically a capacitor) after rectification. Even then, some ripple remains.

Key Educational Points:

  • Half-wave rectifier efficiency is lower than full-wave because it uses only 50% of the input waveform. The output has higher ripple and lower average voltage.
  • The average DC output voltage of an ideal half-wave rectifier: VDC = Vpeak / π ≈ 0.318 x Vpeak.
  • A smoothing capacitor placed in parallel with the load reduces ripple by charging during the pulse and discharging during the blocked half-cycle.

Bottom Line: A half-wave rectifier passes one half of the AC cycle and blocks the other using a single diode. The output is pulsating DC (not smooth). Full-wave rectifiers are more efficient because they use both halves of the AC cycle.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: The depletion region at a PN junction forms because:
options: External voltage is applied to the junction
Majority carriers diffuse across the junction, leaving behind immobile ionized dopant atoms
Photons are absorbed at the junction, creating electron-hole pairs
The temperature gradient across the junction drives carriers apart
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Majority carriers diffuse across the junction, leaving behind immobile ionized dopant atoms

When P-type and N-type semiconductors are joined, a concentration gradient drives diffusion of majority carriers across the junction: electrons diffuse from N to P, and holes diffuse from P to N. As these mobile carriers leave their respective regions, they leave behind fixed, immobile ionized dopant atoms: positive donor ions (like P+) on the N-side and negative acceptor ions (like B-) on the P-side. This region of exposed fixed charges, depleted of mobile carriers, is the depletion region.

The fixed charges create a built-in electric field that opposes further diffusion, establishing equilibrium.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. External voltage is applied to the junction: The depletion region forms spontaneously when the PN junction is created, without any external voltage. In fact, external voltage modifies the already-existing depletion region (forward bias narrows it, reverse bias widens it), but it does not create it.

C. Photons are absorbed at the junction, creating electron-hole pairs: Photon absorption creates electron-hole pairs in solar cells and photodiodes, but this is not the mechanism that forms the depletion region. The depletion region exists in the dark, formed purely by carrier diffusion and the resulting charge imbalance.

D. The temperature gradient across the junction drives carriers apart: There is no inherent temperature gradient across a PN junction that forms the depletion region. Temperature affects carrier mobility and generation rates uniformly. While the thermoelectric (Seebeck) effect can create voltage from temperature gradients, this is a different phenomenon entirely.

Key Educational Points:

  • The built-in potential (contact potential) across the depletion region is about 0.7 V for silicon and 0.3 V for germanium at room temperature.
  • The depletion region width is typically on the order of 0.1 to 1 micrometer.
  • Equilibrium is reached when the drift current (due to the electric field) exactly balances the diffusion current (due to the concentration gradient).

Bottom Line: The depletion region forms spontaneously by diffusion of majority carriers across the PN junction, exposing fixed ionized dopant atoms. This creates a built-in electric field that establishes equilibrium. No external voltage is needed.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: The Bernoulli equation for an ideal fluid flowing in a horizontal pipe states that the sum of which quantities remains constant along a streamline?
options: Pressure energy, kinetic energy, and thermal energy
Static pressure, dynamic pressure (1/2 ρ v<sup>2</sup>), and hydrostatic pressure (ρ g h)
Pressure, velocity, and viscous shear stress
Gravitational potential energy, surface tension energy, and kinetic energy
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Static pressure, dynamic pressure (1/2 ρ v2), and hydrostatic pressure (ρ g h)

The Bernoulli equation expresses conservation of energy per unit volume along a streamline for an ideal (inviscid, incompressible) fluid:

P + (1/2) ρ v2 + ρ g h = constant

where:

  • P = static pressure (pressure energy per unit volume)
  • (1/2) ρ v2 = dynamic pressure (kinetic energy per unit volume)
  • ρ g h = hydrostatic pressure (gravitational potential energy per unit volume)

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Pressure energy, kinetic energy, and thermal energy: Bernoulli's equation applies to ideal fluids with no viscosity. Thermal energy (internal energy) is not included because there is no friction converting mechanical energy to heat. The equation is a statement of mechanical energy conservation only.

C. Pressure, velocity, and viscous shear stress: Bernoulli's equation explicitly assumes zero viscosity (ideal fluid). Viscous shear stress is not a term in the Bernoulli equation. When viscosity is important, the Navier-Stokes equations must be used instead.

D. Gravitational potential energy, surface tension energy, and kinetic energy: Surface tension is not a term in the Bernoulli equation. Surface tension effects are relevant at free surfaces and interfaces (capillarity, droplet formation) but are not part of the energy balance expressed by Bernoulli's principle.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Bernoulli equation explains why fluid velocity increases through a constriction (and pressure decreases) -- this is the Venturi effect, used in medical nebulizers and Venturi masks.
  • Assumptions required: incompressible fluid, steady (non-turbulent) flow, no viscosity, flow along a streamline.
  • Clinical applications: arterial stenosis increases blood velocity and decreases lateral pressure (contributing to vessel collapse in severe stenosis); airplane wing lift; Pitot tubes for measuring fluid velocity.

Bottom Line: Bernoulli equation: P + (1/2)ρ v2 + ρ g h = constant. The three terms are static pressure, dynamic pressure, and hydrostatic pressure. Higher velocity means lower pressure -- this is the core of Bernoulli's principle.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A cyclotron cannot accelerate neutrons because:
options: Neutrons are too heavy to reach relativistic speeds
Neutrons have no electric charge and cannot be deflected by magnetic or accelerated by electric fields
Neutrons decay too quickly to complete a full revolution
The magnetic field of a cyclotron repels neutral particles
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Neutrons have no electric charge and cannot be deflected by magnetic or accelerated by electric fields

A cyclotron operates by using a magnetic field to bend charged particles into circular paths and an oscillating electric field between two D-shaped electrodes ("dees") to accelerate them at each half-revolution. Both mechanisms require the particle to carry an electric charge:

  • The magnetic Lorentz force (F = qvB) depends on charge q; if q = 0, there is no force.
  • The electric force (F = qE) in the accelerating gap also depends on charge q.

Since neutrons are electrically neutral (q = 0), neither force acts on them, making cyclotron acceleration impossible.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Neutrons are too heavy to reach relativistic speeds: The neutron mass (1.675 x 10-27 kg) is very similar to the proton mass (1.673 x 10-27 kg), and protons are routinely accelerated in cyclotrons. Mass is not the limiting factor; the absence of charge is.

C. Neutrons decay too quickly to complete a full revolution: Free neutrons do undergo β decay, but their half-life is approximately 10 minutes, which is vastly longer than the microseconds needed for cyclotron revolutions. Decay is not the reason cyclotrons cannot accelerate neutrons.

D. The magnetic field of a cyclotron repels neutral particles: Magnetic fields exert no force on neutral particles, neither attraction nor repulsion. The Lorentz force is F = qv x B; with q = 0, there is zero force. Magnetic fields do not repel neutral particles.

Key Educational Points:

  • Cyclotrons can accelerate protons, deuterons, α particles, and other charged ions.
  • The cyclotron frequency (f = qB/(2 π m)) is independent of the particle's speed and radius (in the non-relativistic limit), which is what makes the cyclotron work with a fixed-frequency oscillator.
  • Neutrons are accelerated indirectly: protons are accelerated and then directed at a target to produce neutron beams via nuclear reactions.

Bottom Line: Cyclotrons require charged particles because both the magnetic force (for circular motion) and the electric force (for acceleration) are proportional to charge. Neutrons (q = 0) experience neither force and cannot be accelerated or steered.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A positive charge moves to the right in a magnetic field directed into the page. The Lorentz force on the charge is directed:
options: Upward (toward the top of the page)
Downward (toward the bottom of the page)
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Upward (toward the top of the page)

The magnetic Lorentz force on a moving charge is given by F = qv x B. Using the right-hand rule for a positive charge:

  1. Point your fingers in the direction of velocity v (to the right)
  2. Curl your fingers in the direction of B (into the page)
  3. Your thumb points in the direction of F (upward)

Alternatively: v (right) x B (into page) → using cross product rules, right x (into page) = up.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Into the page: The force must be perpendicular to both v and B. Since v is to the right (in the plane of the page) and B is into the page, the force must be in the plane of the page (either up or down), not into or out of the page.

B. Out of the page: Same reasoning as above. The force lies in the plane of the page for this geometry. "Out of the page" is parallel to B, but the cross product v x B is always perpendicular to B.

D. Downward: This would be the direction if the charge were negative. For a negative charge, the force is opposite to v x B. This is a critical distinction: the sign of the charge reverses the direction of the magnetic force. Electrons moving to the right in this field would be deflected downward.

Key Educational Points:

  • The Lorentz force is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it changes the direction of motion but does no work (cannot change the particle's speed or kinetic energy).
  • For a negative charge, the force direction is reversed: F = qv x B with q < 0 gives the opposite direction to the right-hand rule result.
  • This principle underlies many technologies: mass spectrometers (separating ions by mass), cyclotrons, cathode ray tubes, and the Hall effect.

Bottom Line: Use the right-hand rule for positive charges: fingers point along v, curl toward B, thumb gives F direction. For negative charges, reverse the result. The magnetic force is always perpendicular to velocity and does no work.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A perfect black body is an object that:
options: Absorbs all incident radiation but emits none
Absorbs all incident radiation and is also the most efficient emitter of thermal radiation
Emits radiation only at a single specific wavelength
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Absorbs all incident radiation and is also the most efficient emitter of thermal radiation

A perfect black body has two equally important properties: it absorbs all electromagnetic radiation that falls on it (no reflection, no transmission) AND it is the most efficient possible emitter of thermal radiation at every wavelength for its given temperature. These properties are linked by Kirchhoff's law of thermal radiation, which states that at thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a body equals its absorptivity.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Absorbs all incident radiation but emits none: This is the most common misconception about black bodies. A perfect absorber that emitted nothing would violate thermodynamics -- its temperature would increase without limit. In fact, a black body at temperature T radiates energy at a rate given by the Stefan-Boltzmann law: P = σ A T4. A black body is both the perfect absorber AND the most powerful emitter at any given temperature.

C. Reflects all incident radiation perfectly: This describes a perfect mirror (or perfect reflector), which is the exact opposite of a black body. A perfect reflector has zero absorptivity and zero emissivity, while a black body has maximum absorptivity and maximum emissivity.

D. Emits radiation only at a single specific wavelength: A black body emits a continuous spectrum of radiation across all wavelengths, with the intensity distribution described by Planck's law. The peak wavelength depends on temperature (Wien's law), but emission occurs at all wavelengths. A single-wavelength emitter would be a laser or a spectral line source.

Key Educational Points:

  • The black body spectrum (Planck curve) resolved the "ultraviolet catastrophe" of classical physics and was one of the foundations of quantum mechanics.
  • Total radiated power: P = σ A T4 (Stefan-Boltzmann law), where σ = 5.67 x 10-8 W/(m2 K4).
  • Real objects approximate black bodies to varying degrees. Stars, ovens, and the cosmic microwave background are good approximations.

Bottom Line: A black body absorbs ALL incident radiation AND radiates more energy than any other object at the same temperature. Good absorbers are good emitters -- this is Kirchhoff's law. The common misconception that black bodies only absorb is wrong.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: According to Wien's displacement law, as the temperature of a black body increases, the wavelength at which it emits maximum radiation:
options: Increases (shifts toward longer wavelengths / red shift)
Decreases (shifts toward shorter wavelengths / blue shift)
First increases then decreases as temperature rises
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Decreases (shifts toward shorter wavelengths / blue shift)

Wien's displacement law states that the peak emission wavelength of a black body is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature:

λmax = b / T

where b = 2.898 x 10-3 m K (Wien's displacement constant) and T is the absolute temperature in kelvin. As temperature increases, λmax decreases (shifts to shorter wavelengths).

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Increases (shifts toward longer wavelengths): This is backwards. Cooler objects emit predominantly at longer wavelengths (infrared), while hotter objects emit at shorter wavelengths (visible, ultraviolet). A red-hot iron bar (shorter peak wavelength) is hotter than a warm radiator (infrared peak).

B. Remains constant regardless of temperature: The peak wavelength is strongly temperature-dependent. This would violate Wien's law and our everyday observation that heated objects change color (dark red → orange → yellow → white → blue-white) as they get hotter.

D. First increases then decreases: The relationship is strictly monotonic (always decreasing with increasing temperature). There is no turning point or non-monotonic behavior in Wien's law.

Key Educational Points:

  • Examples: The Sun (T ≈ 5,800 K) has λmax ≈ 500 nm (green/yellow visible light). The human body (T ≈ 310 K) has λmax ≈ 9,400 nm (infrared). A very hot blue star (T ≈ 30,000 K) peaks at ~97 nm (ultraviolet).
  • This law explains why hotter stars appear blue-white and cooler stars appear red-orange.
  • Infrared thermometry (fever scanners, thermal cameras) works because body temperature determines the peak infrared emission wavelength.

Bottom Line: Wien's law: λmax = b/T. Hotter objects emit at shorter (bluer) wavelengths; cooler objects emit at longer (redder) wavelengths. This is why heating metal makes it glow from red to orange to yellow to white.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: The activity of a radioactive sample as a function of time follows which mathematical relationship?
options: Exponential decrease: A(t) = A0 e^(-λ t)
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Exponential decrease: A(t) = A0 e^(-λ t)

Radioactive decay is a first-order process where the rate of decay is proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei present. This gives rise to an exponential decay function:

A(t) = A0 e^(-λ t)

where A0 is the initial activity, λ is the decay constant, and t is time. Equivalently: N(t) = N0 e^(-λ t) for the number of remaining nuclei.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Linear decrease: A(t) = A0 - kt: A linear decrease would mean the activity drops by a constant amount per unit time, eventually reaching exactly zero and potentially going negative. In reality, each half-life reduces the activity by half of its current value (not a fixed amount), so the decline slows as fewer nuclei remain. Radioactive decay never follows a straight line.

B. Quadratic decrease: A(t) = A0 - kt2: A quadratic decrease would initially be slow, then accelerate -- the opposite of what actually occurs. In radioactive decay, the rate of decrease is fastest at the beginning (when there are the most undecayed nuclei) and progressively slows.

D. Logarithmic decrease: A(t) = A0 ln(t): This expression is mathematically problematic (it is undefined at t = 0 and increases with time for A0 > 0). Logarithmic functions do not describe the temporal behavior of radioactive decay.

Key Educational Points:

  • The half-life (t1/2) is related to the decay constant: t1/2 = ln(2)/lambda ≈ 0.693/lambda.
  • After n half-lives, the remaining activity is: A = A0 x (1/2)n.
  • The exponential nature means radioactive material theoretically never fully decays to zero; it asymptotically approaches zero.
  • Common medical isotopes: Tc-99m (t1/2 = 6 hours), I-131 (t1/2 = 8 days), Co-60 (t1/2 = 5.27 years).

Bottom Line: Radioactive decay is exponential, not linear. A(t) = A0 e^(-λ t). Each half-life halves the remaining activity. The exponential nature is a direct consequence of the decay rate being proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei present.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A radioactive isotope has a decay constant of λ = 0.0693 per day. Its half-life is approximately:
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 10 days

The half-life is calculated from the decay constant using:

t1/2 = ln(2) / λ = 0.693 / λ

Substituting λ = 0.0693 per day:

t1/2 = 0.693 / 0.0693 = 10 days

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. 5 days: This would require λ = 0.693/5 = 0.1386 per day, which is twice the given value. You might get this if you mistakenly used t1/2 = 0.5/lambda or confused the formula.

C. 14.4 days: This would correspond to λ = 0.693/14.4 ≈ 0.0481 per day. This error might arise from using 1/lambda instead of ln(2)/lambda. The mean lifetime (τ) = 1/lambda = 1/0.0693 = 14.4 days. Mean lifetime and half-life are related but not equal: τ = t1/2/ln(2) ≈ 1.443 x t1/2.

D. 69.3 days: This results from incorrectly using t1/2 = ln(2) x (1/lambda) x 100 or simply confusing the formula. This value has no physical meaning in this context.

Key Educational Points:

  • Key relationships: t1/2 = ln(2)/lambda = 0.693/lambda. Mean lifetime: τ = 1/lambda = t1/2/0.693.
  • Do not confuse half-life and mean lifetime. The mean lifetime is always longer than the half-life by a factor of 1/ln(2) ≈ 1.443.
  • Quick check: after one half-life (10 days), 50% of the original sample remains. After two half-lives (20 days), 25% remains. After three (30 days), 12.5% remains.

Bottom Line: t1/2 = 0.693/lambda. For λ = 0.0693/day: t1/2 = 0.693/0.0693 = 10 days. Remember the distinction between half-life (0.693/lambda) and mean lifetime (1/lambda) -- they are different quantities.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A substance is described as isotropic. This means that its physical properties are:
options: Dependent on the temperature but not direction
Uniform only along one specific crystal axis
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: The same in all directions

An isotropic substance has physical properties (such as refractive index, thermal conductivity, electrical conductivity, elasticity, and speed of wave propagation) that are identical in every direction. The prefix "iso-" means equal, and "tropos" means direction.

Why the other choices are wrong:

A. Different in different directions: This defines an anisotropic substance. Anisotropic materials have direction-dependent properties. Examples include many crystals (calcite shows double refraction because its refractive index differs along different crystal axes), wood (stronger along the grain), and skeletal muscle (contracts along one axis).

C. Dependent on the temperature but not direction: While isotropic materials do have properties that change with temperature, temperature dependence is not what defines isotropy. Isotropy specifically refers to directional uniformity. Both isotropic and anisotropic materials can have temperature-dependent properties.

D. Uniform only along one specific crystal axis: If properties are uniform along only one axis, the material is anisotropic with a specific symmetry (possibly uniaxial). An isotropic material must have the same properties in all directions, not just along one axis.

Key Educational Points:

  • Common isotropic materials: amorphous solids (glass), liquids, gases, and cubic crystals (NaCl, diamond) -- these all have the same properties in every direction.
  • Common anisotropic materials: non-cubic crystals (quartz, calcite), wood, biological tissues (muscle, bone), liquid crystals.
  • In biophysics, understanding isotropy vs. anisotropy is important for: ultrasound propagation in tissues, diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) in MRI (which maps anisotropic water diffusion in white matter tracts), and mechanical properties of bone.

Bottom Line: Isotropic = same properties in all directions ("iso" = equal, "tropos" = direction). Anisotropic = different properties in different directions. Gases, liquids, glass, and cubic crystals are isotropic. Most biological tissues and non-cubic crystals are anisotropic.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: An ideal gas with adiabatic index γ = 5/3 is compressed adiabatically from volume 8 L to 1 L. If the initial pressure is 100 kPa, what is the final pressure?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 3200 kPa

For an adiabatic process: p1V1γ = p2V2γ

p2 = p1 × (V1/V2)γ

p2 = 100 × (8/1)5/3

85/3 = (81/3)5 = 25 = 32

p2 = 100 × 32 = 3200 kPa

Note: In an isothermal compression by factor 8, pressure would only increase 8-fold (to 800 kPa). The adiabatic compression gives a larger pressure increase (32-fold) because the gas also heats up during compression.

Key Concept: In adiabatic compression, pVγ = constant. Since γ > 1, pressure rises faster than in an isothermal process. For monatomic gas, γ = 5/3.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: In an adiabatic expansion of an ideal gas, which of the following is TRUE?
options: The gas absorbs heat from the surroundings and cools down
The temperature of the gas decreases because it does work at the expense of internal energy
The temperature remains constant because no heat is exchanged
The internal energy increases because no heat is lost
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: The temperature of the gas decreases because it does work at the expense of internal energy

In an adiabatic process, Q = 0 (no heat exchange). The first law of thermodynamics gives:

ΔU = Q − W = 0 − W = −W

During expansion, the gas does positive work (W > 0), so ΔU is negative: internal energy decreases. Since internal energy is proportional to temperature for an ideal gas, the temperature falls.

ProcessAdiabatic CompressionAdiabatic Expansion
Q00
Work on gasPositive (compressed)Negative (expands)
ΔUIncreasesDecreases
TemperatureRisesFalls

Key Concept: Adiabatic means Q = 0, NOT ΔT = 0. Temperature changes because internal energy is converted to/from work. Adiabatic expansion cools the gas; adiabatic compression heats it.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A 2 kg mass oscillates on a spring with spring constant k = 800 N/m. What is the period of oscillation?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 0.314 s

For a spring-mass system, the period is:

T = 2π × √(m/k)

T = 2 × 3.14 × √(2/800)

T = 6.28 × √(0.0025)

T = 6.28 × 0.05

T = 0.314 s

The angular frequency is ω = 2π/T = 6.28/0.314 = 20 rad/s. You can verify: ω = √(k/m) = √(800/2) = √400 = 20 rad/s.

Key Concept: T = 2π√(m/k) for a spring-mass system. The period does NOT depend on amplitude. Heavier mass = longer period. Stiffer spring (larger k) = shorter period.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A simple pendulum has a period of 2 seconds on Earth (g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>). What is the length of the pendulum?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 1.0 m

For a simple pendulum: T = 2π√(L/g). Solving for L:

T2 = 4π2 × L/g

L = g × T2 / (4π2)

L = 10 × 4 / (4 × 9.87)

L = 40 / 39.48

L ≈ 1.0 m

A pendulum with period of exactly 2 seconds (1 second each way) is called a "seconds pendulum" and has a length of approximately 1 meter on Earth's surface.

Key Concept: T = 2π√(L/g) for a simple pendulum. Period depends on length and g only -- NOT on mass or amplitude (for small angles). Doubling L multiplies T by √2.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: In simple harmonic motion, at which position is the kinetic energy of the oscillator at its maximum?
options: Kinetic energy is constant throughout the motion
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: At the equilibrium position

In SHM, energy continuously converts between kinetic and potential forms:

PositionKEPESpeed
Equilibrium (y = 0)MaximumZeroMaximum
Extreme (y = ±A)ZeroMaximumZero

At equilibrium, all energy is kinetic: KEmax = ½kA2. At the extremes, all energy is potential: PEmax = ½kA2. The total mechanical energy E = KE + PE = ½kA2 is constant throughout.

Key Concept: In SHM, speed is maximum at equilibrium (center) and zero at the extremes (turning points). The total energy is constant, oscillating between KE and PE forms.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A soap bubble has a radius of 5 cm. If the surface tension of the soap solution is 0.04 N/m, what is the excess pressure inside the bubble compared to outside?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 3.2 Pa

A soap bubble has two surfaces (inner and outer), so the Laplace pressure formula is:

Δp = 4σ/r

Δp = 4 × 0.04 / 0.05

Δp = 0.16 / 0.05

Δp = 3.2 Pa

Compare: A liquid droplet of the same size would have Δp = 2σ/r = 2 × 0.04/0.05 = 1.6 Pa (half as much, since it has only one surface).

TypeNumber of SurfacesFormula
Liquid droplet1Δp = 2σ/r
Soap bubble2Δp = 4σ/r

Key Concept: A soap bubble has TWO surfaces (inner and outer film), so excess pressure is 4σ/r (double that of a droplet). Smaller radius means higher internal pressure.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: Water (surface tension 0.073 N/m, density 1000 kg/m<sup>3</sup>, contact angle 0°) rises in a glass capillary tube to a height of 3 cm. What is the approximate inner radius of the tube? (g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>)
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 0.49 mm

Using the capillary rise formula and solving for r:

h = 2σcos(θ) / (ρgr)

r = 2σcos(θ) / (ρgh)

r = 2 × 0.073 × 1 / (1000 × 10 × 0.03)

r = 0.146 / 300

r = 4.87 × 10−4 m

r ≈ 0.49 mm

With cos(0°) = 1 for perfect wetting of water on glass. Note the inverse relationship: narrower tubes produce higher capillary rise.

Key Concept: h = 2σcos(θ)/(ρgr). Capillary height is inversely proportional to tube radius. For water on glass, cos(θ) ≈ 1. For mercury on glass, cos(θ) is negative (depression instead of rise).

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A solenoid has 2000 turns wound uniformly over a length of 0.4 m and carries a current of 3 A. What is the magnetic field inside the solenoid? (μ<sub>0</sub> = 4π × 10<sup>−7</sup> T·m/A)
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 18.85 mT

The magnetic field inside a solenoid is B = μ0 × n × I, where n = N/L:

n = N/L = 2000/0.4 = 5000 turns/m

B = 4π × 10−7 × 5000 × 3

B = 4 × 3.14 × 10−7 × 15000

B = 12.56 × 10−7 × 15000

B = 188400 × 10−7

B = 1.884 × 10−2 T = 18.85 mT

Key Concept: B = μ0nI inside a solenoid. The key factor is turns per unit length (n = N/L), not total turns alone. Doubling the current doubles B. Inserting an iron core can increase B by a factor of 1000 or more.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A solenoid produces a magnetic field of 10 mT when carrying 2 A. If the current is increased to 5 A (with no other changes), the magnetic field becomes:
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 25 mT

Since B = μ0nI, the field is directly proportional to the current:

B2/B1 = I2/I1

B2 = B1 × I2/I1

B2 = 10 × 5/2

B2 = 25 mT

The field scales linearly with current because μ0 and n are constants of the solenoid geometry.

Key Concept: The solenoid field B = μ0nI is directly proportional to current. If you double I, you double B. This linear relationship makes solenoids useful as controllable electromagnets.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: Light of wavelength 310 nm strikes a metal surface with work function W = 3.0 eV. What is the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons? (Use E(eV) = 1240/λ(nm))
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 1.0 eV

Using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

Ephoton = 1240/λ(nm) = 1240/310 = 4.0 eV

KEmax = Ephoton − W

KEmax = 4.0 − 3.0 = 1.0 eV

The photon energy (4.0 eV) exceeds the work function (3.0 eV), so electrons are emitted. The excess energy becomes kinetic energy of the fastest electrons. The stopping voltage would be 1.0 V.

Key Concept: KEmax = hf − W. Use the shortcut E(eV) = 1240/λ(nm) for fast calculations. If Ephoton < W, no electrons are emitted regardless of intensity.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: In the photoelectric effect, if the intensity of light (above threshold frequency) is doubled while keeping the frequency unchanged, what happens?
options: The maximum kinetic energy of electrons doubles
The number of emitted electrons doubles but their maximum kinetic energy stays the same
Both the number and maximum kinetic energy of electrons double
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: The number of emitted electrons doubles but their maximum kinetic energy stays the same

This is a key insight of the quantum (photon) model of light:

ChangeEffect on Number of ElectronsEffect on KEmax
Increase intensity (same f)Increases (more photons)No change
Increase frequency (same intensity)Decreases slightlyIncreases

Doubling intensity means doubling the number of photons hitting the surface per second. Each photon still has the same energy (hf), so each electron still gets the same maximum kinetic energy. But twice as many photons eject twice as many electrons, doubling the photocurrent.

Key Concept: Intensity = number of photons. Frequency = energy per photon. KEmax depends ONLY on frequency (via hf − W). The number of emitted electrons depends on intensity. This is why classical wave theory fails.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A muon has a half-life of 1.5 μs in its own rest frame. If it travels at 0.6c relative to Earth, what is its half-life as measured by an Earth observer?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 1.875 μs

Using the time dilation formula:

t = t0 / √(1 − v2/c2)

t = 1.5 / √(1 − 0.36)

t = 1.5 / √(0.64)

t = 1.5 / 0.8

t = 1.875 μs

The Lorentz factor γ = 1/0.8 = 1.25. The Earth observer sees the muon's clock running slower, so its measured half-life is longer. This effect is real and experimentally verified: cosmic-ray muons reach Earth's surface because their dilated lifetime is long enough for them to survive the journey through the atmosphere.

Key Concept: Time dilation: t = γt0. Moving clocks run SLOW as seen by a stationary observer. The proper time t0 is always the shortest time (measured in the object's own rest frame).

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: How much energy is released if 1 gram of matter is completely converted to energy? (c = 3 × 10<sup>8</sup> m/s)
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 9 × 1013 J

Using Einstein's mass-energy equivalence:

E = mc2

E = 0.001 kg × (3 × 108 m/s)2

E = 10−3 × 9 × 1016

E = 9 × 1013 J

To put this in perspective, 9 × 1013 J is equivalent to about 21.5 kilotons of TNT -- roughly the energy of the Nagasaki atomic bomb. This shows why nuclear reactions (which convert a tiny fraction of mass to energy) release such enormous energy.

Key Concept: E = mc2. Always convert mass to kg first. c2 = 9 × 1016 m2/s2. Even a tiny mass yields enormous energy because c2 is so large.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A uniform 4 m beam of negligible weight is supported at its center. A 40 N weight hangs 1.5 m to the left of the fulcrum. How far to the right of the fulcrum should a 60 N weight be placed to balance the beam?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 1.0 m

For rotational equilibrium, the sum of torques about the fulcrum must equal zero:

Counterclockwise torque = Clockwise torque

F1 × d1 = F2 × d2

40 × 1.5 = 60 × d2

60 = 60 × d2

d2 = 1.0 m

Check: Left torque = 40 × 1.5 = 60 N·m. Right torque = 60 × 1.0 = 60 N·m. They are equal, so the beam is balanced.

The heavier weight (60 N) must be placed closer to the fulcrum than the lighter weight (40 N). This is the principle of the lever.

Key Concept: For balance: F1 × d1 = F2 × d2. The heavier weight sits closer to the fulcrum. Choose the pivot point wisely to simplify the equation.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A 60 kg adult sits 1.5 m from the fulcrum of a seesaw. At what distance from the fulcrum should a 30 kg child sit to balance the seesaw? (g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>)
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 3.0 m

For a balanced seesaw, the torques on each side must be equal:

F1 × d1 = F2 × d2

m1g × d1 = m2g × d2

60 × 1.5 = 30 × d2

90 = 30 × d2

d2 = 3.0 m

Since g appears on both sides, it cancels out. The child (half the mass) must sit twice as far from the fulcrum. This is the principle of the lever: a smaller force can balance a larger force by acting at a greater distance.

Key Concept: Balanced seesaw: m1 × d1 = m2 × d2 (g cancels). Half the weight needs double the distance. This is Archimedes' lever principle.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: How long must a current of 10 A flow through a solution of AgNO<sub>3</sub> to deposit 5.4 g of silver? (M<sub>Ag</sub> = 108 g/mol, z = 1, F = 96500 C/mol)
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 483 s

Using Faraday's first law, solved for time:

m = (M × I × t) / (z × F)

t = (m × z × F) / (M × I)

t = (5.4 × 1 × 96500) / (108 × 10)

t = 521100 / 1080

t ≈ 483 s (about 8 minutes)

Check: moles of Ag = 5.4/108 = 0.05 mol. Since z = 1, we need 0.05 mol of electrons. Charge = 0.05 × 96500 = 4825 C. Time = Q/I = 4825/10 = 482.5 s. Consistent.

Key Concept: m = MIt/(zF). For Ag+, z = 1 (one electron per ion). Always convert the formula to solve for the unknown. Total charge Q = It is the fundamental quantity in electrolysis.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: The same quantity of electric charge is passed through solutions of CuSO<sub>4</sub> and AgNO<sub>3</sub> connected in series. If 2.16 g of silver is deposited, how much copper is deposited? (M<sub>Ag</sub> = 108, z<sub>Ag</sub> = 1; M<sub>Cu</sub> = 64, z<sub>Cu</sub> = 2)
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 0.64 g

By Faraday's second law, when the same charge passes through different electrolytes in series:

mCu/mAg = (MCu/zCu) / (MAg/zAg)

mCu/2.16 = (64/2) / (108/1)

mCu/2.16 = 32/108

mCu = 2.16 × 32/108

mCu = 2.16 × 0.2963

mCu = 0.64 g

The electrochemical equivalent of Cu (M/z = 64/2 = 32) is less than that of Ag (108/1 = 108), so less copper is deposited for the same charge.

Key Concept: For electrolytes in series (same charge), the ratio of deposited masses equals the ratio of their electrochemical equivalents M/z. Higher valence (z) means less mass deposited per unit charge.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: An electron is accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 400 V. What is its de Broglie wavelength? (Use the shortcut: λ(nm) = 1.226/√V for electrons)
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 0.0613 nm

Using the electron wavelength shortcut:

λ(nm) = 1.226 / √V

λ = 1.226 / √400

λ = 1.226 / 20

λ = 0.0613 nm

This is about 0.6 angstroms, comparable to atomic spacings in crystals. This is why electron diffraction works and why electron microscopes can resolve individual atoms.

Full derivation check: KE = eV = 1.602 × 10−19 × 400 = 6.408 × 10−17 J. v = √(2KE/m) = √(2 × 6.408 × 10−17/9.109 × 10−31) = 1.186 × 107 m/s. λ = h/(mv) = 6.626 × 10−34/(9.109 × 10−31 × 1.186 × 107) = 6.13 × 10−11 m = 0.0613 nm. Matches.

Key Concept: For electrons: λ(nm) = 1.226/√V. Higher accelerating voltage = shorter wavelength = better resolution. This is why transmission electron microscopes use voltages of 100-300 kV.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: An X-ray tube operates at 40 kV. What is the shortest wavelength X-ray it can produce? (Use: λ<sub>min</sub>(nm) = 1.240/V(kV))
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 0.031 nm

The minimum wavelength corresponds to the maximum energy photon, produced when an electron converts ALL its kinetic energy into a single photon:

λmin(nm) = 1.240 / V(kV)

λmin = 1.240 / 40

λmin = 0.031 nm

Full calculation: Emax = eV = 1.602 × 10−19 × 40000 = 6.408 × 10−15 J. λmin = hc/E = (6.626 × 10−34 × 3 × 108)/6.408 × 10−15 = 3.1 × 10−11 m = 0.031 nm. Matches.

This is called the short-wavelength cutoff. No X-ray from this tube can have a wavelength shorter than this value, because it would require more energy than the electron possesses.

Key Concept: λmin(nm) = 1.240/V(kV). Higher voltage produces more energetic (shorter wavelength) X-rays. The continuous spectrum (bremsstrahlung) has a sharp cutoff at λmin.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A 10 μF capacitor is connected to a 50 Hz AC source with peak voltage V<sub>0</sub> = 100 V. What is the capacitive reactance and the peak current?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: XC = 318 Ω, I0 = 0.314 A

The capacitive reactance (opposition to AC current by a capacitor) is:

XC = 1 / (2πfC)

XC = 1 / (2 × 3.14 × 50 × 10 × 10−6)

XC = 1 / (2 × 3.14 × 50 × 10−5)

XC = 1 / (3.14 × 10−3)

XC = 318 Ω

The peak current:

I0 = V0 / XC = 100 / 318 = 0.314 A

In a purely capacitive AC circuit, the current LEADS the voltage by 90° (a quarter cycle). Higher frequency or larger capacitance means lower reactance and more current.

Key Concept: XC = 1/(2πfC). Capacitive reactance decreases with frequency (capacitors pass high-frequency signals easily). In a capacitor, current leads voltage by 90°. Compare with inductive reactance XL = 2πfL, which increases with frequency.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: At 25 degrees C, the saturation vapor pressure of water is 3.17 kPa. If the actual water vapor pressure in the air is 1.90 kPa, what is the relative humidity?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 60%

Relative humidity (RH) is the ratio of the actual vapor pressure to the saturation vapor pressure at the same temperature:

RH = (pvapor / psaturated) x 100%

RH = (1.90 / 3.17) x 100% = 59.9%, rounding to 60%.

At 100% RH the air is fully saturated and condensation begins (dew, fog). Comfortable indoor humidity is typically 40-60%.

Key Concept: Relative humidity compares actual water vapor content to the maximum the air can hold at that temperature. Warm air can hold more moisture than cold air.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: What happens when air is cooled below its dew point temperature?
options: Water vapor condenses into liquid droplets
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Water vapor condenses into liquid droplets

The dew point is the temperature at which the air becomes fully saturated (RH = 100%). Cooling below this temperature causes the excess water vapor to condense into liquid droplets.

This is why dew forms on grass at night, why glasses fog up when moving from cold to warm air, and why clouds form when moist air rises and cools.

Key Concept: Below the dew point, the excess moisture condenses, keeping RH at 100%.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: Two long parallel wires are 0.2 m apart, each carrying a current of 5 A in the same direction. What is the force per unit length between them?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 2.5 × 10⁻⁵ N/m, attractive

The force per unit length between two parallel current-carrying wires is:

F/L = (μ₀ × I₁ × I₂) / (2πd)

F/L = (4π × 10⁻⁷ × 5 × 5) / (2π × 0.2) = 2.5 × 10⁻⁵ N/m

Currents in the same direction attract; currents in opposite directions repel.

Key Concept: This force between parallel conductors was historically used to define the ampere.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: The SI unit ampere was historically defined using which physical setup?
options: Force between two parallel current-carrying conductors
Deflection of an electron in a magnetic field
EMF produced by a changing magnetic flux
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Force between two parallel current-carrying conductors

The ampere was originally defined as: the constant current which, if maintained in two straight parallel conductors of infinite length and negligible cross-section placed 1 meter apart in vacuum, produces a force of exactly 2 × 10⁻⁷ N per meter of length between them.

Key Concept: The force per unit length between parallel wires carrying current I is F/L = μ₀I²/(2πd). Setting I = 1 A and d = 1 m gives F/L = 2 × 10⁻⁷ N/m.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A straight conductor of length 0.4 m moves at 3 m/s perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of 0.5 T. What EMF is induced across its ends?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: 0.6 V

When a conductor moves through a magnetic field, the motional EMF is:

EMF = B × L × v (when motion is perpendicular to B)

EMF = 0.5 × 0.4 × 3 = 0.6 V

This is the principle behind electric generators: a conductor moving through a magnetic field generates a voltage.

Key Concept: Motional EMF = BLv. This is a special case of Faraday's law where the flux changes because the conductor physically moves.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: The mass of a helium-4 nucleus is 4.0026 u, while the total mass of 2 free protons and 2 free neutrons is 4.0320 u. What does this mass difference represent?
options: Energy absorbed when the nucleus formed
Mass converted to nuclear binding energy via E = mc²
Energy lost to radiation during measurement
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Mass converted to nuclear binding energy via E = mc²

The mass of a nucleus is always less than the sum of its individual nucleons. This mass defect (Δm = 0.0294 u) has been converted into binding energy:

E = Δm × 931.5 MeV/u = 0.0294 × 931.5 = 27.4 MeV

Key Concept: The mass defect represents the energy released when nucleons combined to form the nucleus. This same energy would be needed to break it apart.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: Which nucleus has the highest binding energy per nucleon and is therefore the most stable?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Iron-56

Iron-56 sits at the peak of the binding energy per nucleon curve (~8.8 MeV/nucleon), making it the most tightly bound and most stable nucleus.

Nuclei lighter than iron release energy by fusion. Nuclei heavier than iron release energy by fission. Both processes move toward the iron peak.

Key Concept: The binding energy curve peaks at iron-56. Both fusion and fission release energy because both move toward more stable configurations near iron.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: In a nuclear fission reactor, what is the primary function of the moderator?
options: To absorb excess neutrons and prevent supercriticality
To slow fast neutrons to thermal speeds so they can trigger further fission
To shield workers from γ radiation
To cool the reactor core and prevent overheating
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: To slow fast neutrons to thermal speeds so they can trigger further fission

The moderator (typically water, heavy water, or graphite) slows fast neutrons to thermal energies. Thermal neutrons are much more likely to cause fission in U-235.

Control rods (boron/cadmium) absorb excess neutrons. Shielding blocks radiation. Coolant carries away heat.

Key Concept: Moderator = slows neutrons. Control rods = absorb neutrons. The moderator works because neutrons lose the most energy in collisions with particles of similar mass (hydrogen in water).

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: Which material is most effective at shielding against neutron radiation?
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Water or paraffin

Neutrons are uncharged, so they pass easily through dense materials like lead. Neutrons are best slowed and absorbed by materials containing hydrogen (water, paraffin, concrete).

This is because energy transfer in a collision is maximized when the target has the same mass as the projectile. A hydrogen nucleus (proton) has nearly the same mass as a neutron.

Key Concept: Lead stops γ rays. Hydrogen-rich materials stop neutrons. Different radiation types require different shielding strategies.

physics.subject: physics
physics.question: A specific nuclear species defined by its atomic number Z and mass number A is called a:
physics.explanation:
Correct Answer: Nuclide

A nuclide is a specific nuclear species characterized by its atomic number Z and mass number A.

Isotopes are nuclides with the same Z but different A (same element, different neutrons). A nucleon is a proton or neutron. A nucleotide is a biochemistry term (DNA/RNA building block).

Key Concept: Nuclide = specific Z and A. Isotopes = same Z, different A. Isobars = same A, different Z.
